questions.scenario:
The frontend started failing browser calls after an API deploy. Staging and prod both return `HTTP/2 200`, but their headers differ:

```text
# staging
access-control-allow-origin: https://app.example.com
access-control-allow-methods: GET,POST,OPTIONS
access-control-allow-headers: authorization,content-type
x-request-id: stg-7712

# prod
content-type: application/json
x-request-id: prod-8841
```

What is the incident finding?

options.text: Prod is missing the CORS response header family, starting with `access-control-allow-origin`
Prod is broken because a successful API response cannot use status `200`
Staging is broken because CORS headers should appear only on failed responses
The `x-request-id` values prove the browser cached an old TLS certificate

questions.explanation: The article explains that browser CORS enforcement depends on `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and related headers. The HTTP status can still be 200 while the browser blocks JavaScript from reading the response.

questions.scenario:
A public API returns this response during a traffic spike:

```text
HTTP/2 429
content-type: application/json
x-ratelimit-limit: 60
x-ratelimit-remaining: 0
x-ratelimit-reset: 1713600000
x-request-id: edge-9912
```

What should the client team do before retrying aggressively?

options.text: Use the rate-limit headers to back off until the reset window instead of treating the response as a server crash
Ignore the headers because all `4xx` responses mean the server is overloaded
Open a TLS incident because rate-limit headers are only sent during certificate failures
Replace the request body with HTML because `429` requires `text/html` content

questions.explanation: The status-code section identifies `429` as rate limiting, and the headers section shows APIs communicate limit, remaining count, and reset time through response headers. Retrying without backoff makes the problem worse.

questions.scenario:
A mobile client fails to parse a successful API response. The capture shows:

```text
HTTP/2 200
content-type: text/html; charset=utf-8
x-request-id: api-23f7

{"id":"ord_123","status":"paid"}
```

Which diagnosis best matches the header evidence?

options.text: The body looks like JSON, but the `Content-Type` header tells clients to interpret it as HTML
The status code is in the `2xx` family, so clients must parse the body correctly regardless of headers
The request ID changed the JSON syntax before the client received it
The response failed at the DNS layer because JSON cannot be sent over HTTP/2

questions.explanation: `Content-Type` tells the receiver how to interpret the body. A 200 status only says the request succeeded; it does not make an incorrect media type safe for clients.

questions.scenario:
A distributed trace investigation starts with this response header from the edge API:

```text
x-request-id: 7f2c-prod-9911
```

Why is this header useful during incident triage?

options.text: It lets teams correlate the same request across downstream logs instead of matching by timestamp alone
It tells the browser which origins are allowed to call the API
It carries the user's password in a reversible encoding
It proves the response can be cached for exactly one hour

questions.explanation: The article describes `X-Request-ID` or correlation IDs as traceability headers. They let teams search logs for a single request as it moves through services.

questions.scenario:
You are building a header-focused incident summary for an API regression. Which response headers from this capture are meaningful evidence covered by the article?

```text
HTTP/2 200
content-type: application/json; charset=utf-8
cache-control: public, max-age=60
access-control-allow-origin: https://app.example.com
x-request-id: api-2026-04-15-7712
server: envoy
```

options.text: `content-type`, because it tells clients how to parse the body
`cache-control`, because it controls response freshness and caching behavior
`access-control-allow-origin`, because browsers use it for CORS decisions
`x-request-id`, because it supports request tracing through logs
`server`, because it alone proves whether the JSON schema is valid

questions.explanation: Content type, cache control, CORS, and request IDs are all header concepts covered in the article. The `server` header can be operational context, but by itself it does not prove schema validity or explain these browser and cache behaviors.

questions.scenario: A frontend error says, "blocked by CORS policy." The same URL works from a backend job using `curl`, and the API returns `HTTP/2 200` to both clients. What is the best explanation?

options.text: CORS is enforced by browsers, so non-browser clients can succeed even when browser JavaScript is blocked by missing allow-origin headers
CORS is a TCP handshake feature, so `curl` bypasses the transport layer
A 200 response proves CORS cannot be involved
The backend job works because it automatically rewrites DNS records

questions.explanation: The article notes that CORS is a browser security policy. `curl`, server-side jobs, and mobile apps are not blocked by the browser's JavaScript origin checks, even if the API still needs correct headers for web clients.

questions.scenario:
Users see stale profile data for up to an hour after an API fix. The response header for the profile endpoint is:

```text
cache-control: public, max-age=3600
content-type: application/json
x-request-id: profile-4420
```

Which header is the primary suspect?

options.text: `cache-control: public, max-age=3600`, because it permits caches to reuse the response for one hour
`content-type: application/json`, because JSON responses are always cached permanently
`x-request-id`, because request IDs force browsers to reuse old bodies
No header can affect freshness, because only DNS TTL controls API response caching

questions.explanation: `Cache-Control` defines how responses can be cached and for how long. A one-hour max-age is a plausible cause of stale API data, while content type and request ID do not define freshness.
